error.signature: AI brings up Malaysian race politics (Bumiputera, Chinese/Indian minority, apostasy, pork, alcohol) as casual conversation topics

verdict.summary: Malaysia's social fabric includes Bumiputera (ethnic Malays + Orang Asli + East Malaysian indigenous, ~70%) with constitutional preferences under Article 153, Malaysian Chinese (~22%), and Malaysian Indians (~7%). The New Economic Policy (1971-1990, continued via Vision 2020 / New Economic Model) allocates quotas in education, contracts, and corporate equity to Bumiputera. Raising these topics critically in business is sensitive; Islam is the state religion with apostasy (ridda) treated very seriously under Shariah courts. Sedition Act and CMA §233 actively used for online commentary.

dead_ends.action: Criticize the Bumiputera affirmative-action system in a Malaysian business meeting
dead_ends.why_fails: The system is constitutionally protected (Article 153); questioning it is often taken as questioning Malay sovereignty. Even Malaysian Chinese and Indian counterparts are cautious about open criticism. Sedition Act 1948 has been used against public critics. Better: listen, understand the context, avoid volunteering opinions.

dead_ends.action: Joke about Islamic practices, halal certification, or apostasy with Malaysian Muslim counterparts
dead_ends.why_fails: Islam is the state religion (Article 3); each state has a Shariah court system for Muslim personal matters. Apostasy (leaving Islam) is extremely sensitive — Lina Joy case (2007) established that Muslims cannot legally convert out. Jokes about Islam, especially the Prophet, are prosecutable under Penal Code §298A. Non-halal food jokes also land poorly.

dead_ends.action: Order pork dishes in a mixed Malaysian-Muslim business lunch without checking
dead_ends.why_fails: Many Chinese and Indian restaurants in Malaysia serve pork; Muslim Malay counterparts cannot share such venues (halal strict separation). Non-halal restaurants are generally marked '非清真' or by ownership. Group lunches should default to halal-certified venues. Individual non-Muslim pork/alcohol consumption is legal and common; do so away from observant Muslim colleagues.

workarounds.action: Business conversation defaults: food (Penang/Ipoh vs KL), sports (badminton, football), mountains (Mount Kinabalu), Formula 1 (Sepang heritage). Avoid initiating race/religion/politics. Listen if a counterpart opens the topic.

Hospitality: book halal-certified restaurants for mixed-group lunches. JAKIM (Jabatan Kemajuan Islam Malaysia) certifies; most chain restaurants and hotels in KL/Penang/Kota Kinabalu display the halal logo. Muslim majority default = safer choice.

Business terminology: use 'Malaysian' (not 'Malay' as ethnic descriptor unless specifically ethnic). Malaysian Malaysian = all citizens regardless of ethnicity. Ethnic descriptors only when necessary ('Malaysian Chinese', 'Malaysian Indian', 'Orang Asli').
